title: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)
content: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is the toughest privacy and security law in the world. Though it was drafted and passed by the European Union (EU), it imposes obligations onto organizations anywhere, so long as they target or collect data related to people in the EU. The regulation was put into effect on May 25, 2018. The GDPR levies harsh fines against those who violate its privacy and security standards, with penalties reaching tens of millions of euros. Core principles include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity, and confidentiality. It grants data subjects rights to access, rectification, erasure ('right to be forgotten'), restrict processing, and data portability. Article 33 mandates reporting a data breach to a supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of it. Fines can reach up to €20 million, or 4% of the firm’s worldwide annual revenue from the preceding financial year, whichever amount is higher.

title: European Union Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)
content: The EU AI Act establishes a uniform legal framework for the development, market placement, and use of Artificial Intelligence systems in the European Union. It categorizes AI systems by risk: Unacceptable Risk (banned, e.g., social scoring, real-time remote biometric identification), High Risk (strictly regulated, e.g., critical infrastructure, employment, medical devices), Limited Risk (transparency obligations, e.g., chatbots), and Minimal Risk. High-risk systems must undergo rigorous conformity assessments, maintain high-quality datasets to prevent bias, ensure human oversight, and provide detailed technical documentation. Generative AI models like ChatGPT must comply with transparency requirements, disclose that content was AI-generated, design models to prevent generating illegal content, and publish summaries of copyrighted data used for training. Penalties for non-compliance can reach €35 million or 7% of global turnover for prohibited AI practices, and €15 million or 3% for violating most other obligations.

title: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
content: HIPAA is a federal law that required the creation of national standards to protect sensitive patient health information from being disclosed without the patient’s consent or knowledge. The US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) issued the HIPAA Privacy Rule to implement the requirements of HIPAA. The Privacy Rule standards address the use and disclosure of individuals' health information (known as protected health information or PHI) by entities subject to the Privacy Rule. These individuals and organizations are called 'covered entities'. The HIPAA Security Rule protects a subset of information covered by the Privacy Rule, which is all individually identifiable health information a covered entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits in electronic form. This information is called electronic protected health information (e-PHI). The Security Rule does not apply to PHI transmitted orally or in writing. Violations can result in civil monetary penalties up to $1.5 million per year for identical violations.

title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) / CPRA
content: The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: the right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; the right to delete personal information collected from them; the right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information; and the right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. Businesses that violate the CCPA can be subject to civil penalties up to $2,500 for each violation or $7,500 for each intentional violation.

title: ISO/IEC 27001 Information Security Management
content: ISO/IEC 27001 is an international standard to manage information security. The standard details requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining and continually improving an information security management system (ISMS). The ISMS preserves the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information by applying a risk management process and gives confidence to interested parties that risks are adequately managed. It is part of the ISO/IEC 27000 family of standards, published jointly by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC). Compliance demonstrates to customers and stakeholders that an organization takes security seriously and has robust processes in place to identify, mitigate, and monitor security threats. The 2022 update introduces new controls related to threat intelligence, information security for use of cloud services, ICT readiness for business continuity, and physical security monitoring.

title: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act)
content: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), passed in August 2023, provides a comprehensive framework for the processing of digital personal data. It applies to the processing of personal data collected online or collected offline and digitized. It also applies extraterritorially if data processing relates to offering goods or services to individuals in India. Key concepts include Data Fiduciaries (entities determining purpose and means of processing) and Data Principals (individuals whose data is processed). The law mandates clear, itemized consent in local languages, and places strong emphasis on the protection of children's data, prohibiting behavioral monitoring and targeted advertising directed at children. The Act establishes the Data Protection Board of India for adjudication. Unlike the GDPR, it does not mandate data localization strictly but allows transfers to all countries except those explicitly restricted by the government. Penalties for non-compliance are severe, up to ₹250 crore (approx. $30 million USD) for failing to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a data breach.

title: Prevention of Sexual Harassment (POSH) at Workplace Act
content: The Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, commonly known as the POSH Act, is an Indian law enacted with the objective of making workplaces safer for women. It mandates that every employer with 10 or more employees must constitute an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) to address complaints of sexual harassment. The Act provides a civil remedy and outlines a clear mechanism for redressal, requiring inquiries to be completed within 90 days. Employers are obligated to organize regular awareness workshops, display penal consequences of sexual harassment conspicuously, and include information regarding POSH compliance in their annual reports. Failure to comply, including the non-constitution of an ICC, can result in a fine of ₹50,000, and repeated offenses may lead to the cancellation of the business license.

title: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL)
content: The Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) is China's first comprehensive legislation on personal data protection, heavily influencing global privacy standards. Similar to the GDPR, it has extraterritorial scope, applying to the processing of personal information of individuals within China by foreign entities. The PIPL strictly regulates cross-border data transfers, requiring security assessments by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) or standard contractual clauses before data can leave the country. It establishes strict rules for handling 'sensitive personal information' (e.g., biometrics, medical health, financial accounts) and emphasizes localized storage for critical information infrastructure operators. It also addresses algorithmic transparency, prohibiting automated decision-making from implementing unreasonable differential treatment in pricing. Fines for severe violations can reach 50 million RMB or up to 5% of the organization's revenue from the prior year, alongside business suspensions.

title: Federal Law No. 152-FZ on Personal Data
content: Russia’s Federal Law No. 152-FZ is the primary legislation regulating the processing of personal data in the Russian Federation. A cornerstone requirement, heavily enforced since 2015 via an localization amendment, is that operators collecting personal data of Russian citizens must record, systematize, accumulate, store, clarify (update, change), and extract such data using databases located strictly within the territory of the Russian Federation. Cross-border data transfers are permitted only after primary localization in Russia, and recent amendments mandate notifying the regulatory authority (Roskomnadzor) prior to transferring data abroad. The law grants subjects rights regarding consent, access, and deletion. Non-compliance, particularly regarding data localization rules, can result in domain blocking (the website becoming inaccessible in Russia) and significant administrative fines reaching up to 18 million rubles for repeat offenses.

title: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD)
content: The Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados (LGPD) is Brazil's overarching data protection law, significantly inspired by the EU GDPR. It unites over 40 different statutes that previously governed personal data in Brazil. The LGPD requires processing of personal data to rely on one of ten legal bases, notably including the 'legitimate interest' of the controller. It grants data subjects nine fundamental rights, including the right to data portability, anonymization, and the deletion of unnecessary data. Companies must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) (Encarregado) and report security incidents to the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) within a reasonable time. The law applies to any processing operation carried out in Brazil, regardless of where the operator is headquartered. Fines can reach up to 2% of a private legal entity's revenue in Brazil, capped at 50 million reais per infraction.

title: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA)
content: The Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) is South Africa’s comprehensive data privacy legislation. Uniquely among many global privacy laws, POPIA protects the personal information of not just living individuals (natural persons) but also existing companies and legal entities (juristic persons). It sets out eight conditions for lawful data processing, including accountability, processing limitation, purpose specification, and security safeguards. POPIA strictly regulates direct marketing by means of unsolicited electronic communications, shifting the default to an opt-in regime. Organizations must appoint an Information Officer and register them with the Information Regulator prior to taking up their duties. The Act imposes severe penalties for non-compliance, including fines of up to 10 million ZAR or imprisonment for up to 10 years.

title: Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021 on Personal Data Protection
content: The UAE Personal Data Protection Law (PDPL) represents the first comprehensive federal data privacy legislation in the United Arab Emirates. It applies to processing personal data by controllers and processors within the UAE, as well as foreign entities processing data of data subjects within the UAE. The law establishes the UAE Data Office to regulate and enforce compliance. It relies heavily on consent as the primary legal basis for processing, with specific exemptions where processing is necessary to protect public interest or perform a contract. The PDPL places strict requirements on cross-border data transfers, allowing transfers only to jurisdictions providing an adequate level of protection or through standard contractual clauses. Distinctively, the law excludes government data and health/credit data already governed by sector-specific legislation. It mandates robust breach notification procedures and the appointment of a Data Protection Officer for large-scale or high-risk processing.

title: Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI)
content: Japan’s Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI) is one of Asia's oldest data privacy laws, continuously amended to align with global standards like the GDPR. The law applies to any 'Personal Information Handling Business Operator' (PIHBO) providing services to individuals in Japan. The APPI requires organizations to explicitly state the purpose of data utilization and restricts changing this purpose without consent. It enforces strict rules on transferring data to third parties, especially cross-border transfers, requiring either the data subject's consent or ensuring the recipient country has equivalent standards (Japan was the first country to receive an adequacy decision from the EU). Recent amendments strengthened data breach notification obligations, requiring reports to the Personal Information Protection Commission (PPC) within tight deadlines. The law uniquely categorizes 'pseudonymized information' to foster innovation in healthcare and statistical research while protecting privacy.

title: Privacy Act 1988 (including Notifiable Data Breaches scheme)
content: The Privacy Act 1988 is the principal piece of Australian legislation protecting the handling of personal information about individuals. It regulates Australian Government agencies and organizations with an annual turnover of more than $3 million. At the heart of the Act are the 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), which cover standards, rights, and obligations around the collection, use, disclosure, and storage of personal information. The Act includes the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme, making it mandatory to notify affected individuals and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) when a data breach is likely to result in serious harm. In response to major cyber incidents, recent amendments drastically increased penalties for serious or repeated privacy breaches to the greater of $50 million AUD, three times the value of the benefit obtained, or 30% of the adjusted turnover in the relevant period.

title: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA)
content: Singapore's Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) establishes a data protection law that comprises various rules governing the collection, use, disclosure, and care of personal data. It recognizes both the rights of individuals to protect their personal data and the needs of organizations to collect, use, or disclose personal data for legitimate and reasonable purposes. A key feature of the PDPA is the Do Not Call (DNC) Registry, which allows individuals to register their phone numbers to opt-out of receiving marketing messages. The PDPA operates on principles of consent, purpose limitation, and reasonableness. Recent amendments introduced mandatory data breach notification to the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) and expanded exceptions to consent for legitimate interests and business improvement purposes. Financial penalties for severe breaches can be up to 10% of an organization's annual turnover in Singapore or $1 million SGD, whichever is higher.
